text: The laparoscopic view reveals the left ovary containing a four-centimeter simple cyst. The surrounding pelvic area shows signs of stage two endometriosis.

The surgeon opens the ovary at its thinnest area using monopolar scissors with a cutting current, attempting to reach the plane between the ovarian cyst and the ovary wall. Right angled forceps assist in opening this plane, revealing the thicker ovarian cortex. The cyst is incidentally entered during the process.

The surgeon drains the fluid from the ovarian cyst and irrigates the pelvis. The outer ovarian wall is further opened, and Maryland dissectors are used to grasp the cyst wall while graspers hold the ovary cortex. The cyst wall is bluntly dissected away from the ovary wall with gentle traction, and thicker tissue bands are cauterized using pinpoint monopolar cautery. The dissection progresses around the edge of the cyst as parts of the cyst wall are freed.

The surgeon continues the dissection around the entire ovarian cyst, carefully separating the ovarian cortex from the cyst wall. The cyst wall is held while the cortex is dissected away, minimizing blood loss by maintaining the proper dissection plane. The surgeon avoids excessive pulling to prevent tearing and bleeding. In cases where the cyst is densely adherent, the cyst wall is opened to facilitate dissection of a single layer from the ovarian cortex.

The surgeon excises the majority of the ovarian cyst, leaving a small one-centimeter portion of the cyst wall attached to the ovarian cortex. Electrocautery is used to ablate the remaining cyst wall to prevent damage to the ovarian cortex.

The surgeon inspects and irrigates the ovarian bed, which appears nearly hemostatic with minimal use of electrocautery. The outer edges of the ovary are re-approximated using a running forovicral suture, which is secured and tied off. The ovary is then wrapped in a sheet of interseed to reduce adhesion formation, marking the completion of the procedure.
